title: Validate: Security Considerations expansion (S1-S16, T8, T11)
description:
## What

Validation gate for security considerations bead (syllago-7c550).

## Validation Checklist

1. **Coverage audit:** Create a checklist of all 16 security findings (S1-S16). For each one, verify it is addressed somewhere in Section 11. Mark any gaps.

S1: Unsigned content trust confusion → covered in?
S2: No revocation model → covered in?
S3: False lineage via derived_from → covered in?
S4: Authorship impersonation → covered in?
S5: Content-type mismatch → covered in?
S6: Name squatting → covered in?
S7: Version rollback → covered in?
S8: TOCTOU gap → covered in?
S9: generated_by spoofing → covered in?
S10: published_at self-reported → covered in?
S11: File permissions excluded → covered in?
S12: Composition bombing → covered in?
S13: Symlink TOCTOU → covered in?
S14: Unicode homoglyphs → covered in?
S15: Extra fields injection → covered in? (may be addressed by T2 allowlist instead)
S16: Sigstore identity confusion → covered in?

2. **No existing text modified:** Sections 11.1–11.7 must be byte-identical to the pre-edit version. Diff against committed version.

3. **Tone check:** New text must not be defensive ("this isn't a problem because..."). Must be honest about limitations ("ACP does not prevent this because...").

4. **No normative requirements:** New Security Considerations text should NOT contain MUST/MUST NOT/SHALL/SHALL NOT. Security Considerations describes risks and mitigations, not requirements.
   Exception: If earlier beads (content hashing, conformance) already added normative security text, that's fine — this bead should only add descriptive/advisory text.

5. **Cross-references valid:** Any cross-references to other sections (e.g., "see Section 5.2.2", "see Section 7 Implementation Note") must point to sections that actually exist after all beads are applied.

6. **Deprecation deferral:** Must explicitly state that deprecation/yanking is deferred to registries with rationale.

7. **Offline Sigstore:** Must mention SSH as offline alternative for air-gapped environments.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All 16 security findings accounted for
- [ ] Sections 11.1–11.7 unchanged
- [ ] Tone is honest and non-defensive
- [ ] No new normative requirements in Security Considerations (descriptive only)
- [ ] Cross-references point to real sections
- [ ] Validator is a different agent than implementer
close_reason: 7/7 checks PASS. All 16 security findings covered, Sections 11.1-11.7 unchanged, tone honest, no improper normative requirements, cross-references valid.

title: ACP Spec: Security Considerations expansion (S1-S16, T8, T11)
description:
## What

Expand Section 11 (Security Considerations) to document known limitations identified by the adversarial panel and LLM reviewers. Also add the offline Sigstore note (T8) and document that deprecation/yanking is deferred to registries (T11). This is the largest single bead but is purely additive — no existing text should be modified, only new subsections added.

## File

docs/spec/skills/acp-spec.md

## Changes

### New subsections to add to Section 11

**11.8 Sensitive Files in Content Directories** (from T6/T7 bead — if not already added there, add here)

Already specified in content hashing bead. If it was placed there, skip. Otherwise add it here.

**11.9 OIDC Identity Verification** (from conformance bead — if not already added there, add here)

Already specified in conformance bead. If it was placed there, skip. Otherwise add it here.

**11.10 Self-Reported Claims Exploitability**

Expand beyond the existing Section 11.3. Document specific attack vectors:
- Authorship impersonation on unsigned content (setting famous authors with valid hashes)
- generated_by spoofing (claiming AI agent that didn't generate the content)
- Content-type mismatch (type: rule but content is executable hook code — type is not validated against content)
- False lineage via derived_from with real source_hash (obtainable by hashing public content)
- published_at is a self-reported timestamp, not independently verifiable

For each: describe the attack, explain why ACP doesn't prevent it (provenance records facts, doesn't authenticate them without signatures), and note that signatures bind claims to an identity.

**11.11 Sidecar Stripping and Downgrade Attacks**

Expand beyond existing Section 11.4:
- Removing meta.yaml entirely (existing)
- Removing just the signature field from meta.yaml — content and meta hashes still verify, content downgrades from signed to unsigned
- No mechanism for publishers to assert "this content MUST be signed" enforceable by consumers (beyond strict consumer conformance)
- Mitigation: strict consumers (Section 5.2.2) defend against full stripping; registries can enforce signature requirements

**11.12 Namespace and Version Attacks**

- Name squatting: no publisher namespacing in the spec. Two publishers can publish different content with the same name, version, and type. Registries SHOULD implement namespacing.
- Version rollback: old versions with valid hashes/signatures can be redistributed. No freshness mechanism or "latest version" authority. Consumers should check registries for the latest version.
- Composition bombing: hundreds of derived_from entries pointing to real trusted content. Tooling SHOULD limit displayed derived_from entries.

**11.13 Platform-Specific Verification Failures**

- Windows CRLF (cross-reference to Implementation Note in Section 7)
- Case-insensitive filesystems: meta.yaml vs META.YAML on macOS/Windows. Implementations on case-insensitive systems SHOULD perform case-sensitive filename matching.
- Unicode homoglyph paths: visually identical filenames using different Unicode codepoints. NFC normalization doesn't collapse homoglyphs. Both files hash and verify normally. Defense is out of scope for ACP.

**11.14 Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU)**

- Content verified at install time but read from disk at runtime. An attacker with filesystem access can modify content after verification.
- Symlink TOCTOU: between checking a symlink target is internal and reading content, attacker swaps target.
- Mitigation is implementation-specific (read-only mounts, runtime re-verification). Outside ACP's scope.

**11.15 Algorithm Migration**

Expand beyond existing Section 11.5:
- When meta_version 2 arrives with a new algorithm, consumers supporting both must be careful: an attacker could force downgrade to meta_version 1 with the weaker algorithm.
- No dual-hash deprecation window is defined. This is acknowledged as a gap for future specification work.
- File permissions are excluded from the hash. An attacker can change permissions (e.g., make scripts executable) without affecting hashes.

**11.16 Registry and Ecosystem Security**

- Deprecation/yanking: no field in ACP for marking a version as unsafe. This is deliberately deferred to registries, which control distribution. ACP records provenance, not lifecycle state.
- Rekor verification: Sigstore consumers that fetch by log_index must verify inclusion proofs. Trusting Rekor responses without verification is vulnerable to a compromised Rekor instance.
- Sigstore requires network access for Rekor lookup. For air-gapped environments, SSH signing (Section 9.3) provides an offline-capable alternative.

### Existing sections to NOT modify

Do NOT change Sections 11.1–11.7. They are fine as-is. The new subsections expand coverage, they don't replace existing text.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] At least 7 new subsections added to Section 11 (numbers may vary if some were added by earlier beads)
- [ ] Each attack vector has: description of the attack, why ACP doesn't prevent it, and any available mitigations
- [ ] Self-reported claims section covers: authorship impersonation, generated_by spoofing, content-type mismatch, false lineage, published_at
- [ ] Sidecar stripping covers both full removal AND signature-only stripping
- [ ] Name squatting, version rollback, and composition bombing documented
- [ ] CRLF cross-references the implementation note in Section 7
- [ ] Case-insensitive filesystem issue documented
- [ ] TOCTOU documented for both content and symlinks
- [ ] Algorithm migration acknowledges the downgrade gap
- [ ] File permissions exclusion mentioned
- [ ] Deprecation/yanking explicitly deferred to registries with rationale
- [ ] Sigstore network requirement mentioned with SSH alternative
- [ ] Sections 11.1–11.7 unchanged
- [ ] No normative requirements added in Security Considerations — all new text is descriptive/advisory
- [ ] The tone matches the existing Security Considerations sections (honest, non-defensive, technically precise)
close_reason: Added Sections 11.8-11.16: sensitive files, OIDC identity verification, self-reported claims exploitability (5 vectors), sidecar stripping/downgrade, namespace/version attacks, platform-specific failures (CRLF/case/homoglyph), TOCTOU, algorithm migration, registry/ecosystem security. All 16 security findings covered.

title: Validate: Schema constraints & field rules (T5, T10, T12)
description:
## What

Validation gate for schema constraints bead (syllago-2tdz1).

## Validation Checklist

1. **Name constraints exist:** Section 6.3.3 must contain: character count limit (1-128), allowed character set, forbidden control characters with Unicode ranges, forbidden path separators.

2. **Schema example still valid:** The example name "code-review" in Section 6.2 must conform to the new constraints (only letters, digits, hyphens — it does).

3. **Authors note:** Section 6.3.6 must contain an informative note about non-normalization. Must show an example of different casing producing different meta_hash.

4. **Version note:** Section 6.3.4 must contain an informative note explaining integer versioning over SemVer. Must mention provenance vs package management distinction.

5. **Version behavior unchanged:** Section 6.3.4 must still say integer, starts at 1, resets on derivation. No behavioral changes.

6. **No over-constraining:** The name constraints must allow Unicode letters (not just ASCII). Names like "コードレビュー" or "révision-code" must be valid per the constraint text.

7. **Consistency:** All new notes use the "(Informative)" label consistently with other notes in Section 6.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All 7 checks pass
- [ ] Validator is a different agent than implementer
close_reason: 7/7 checks PASS. Name constraints, authors note, version note all verified.

title: ACP Spec: Schema constraints & field rules (T5, T10, T12)
description:
## What

Add name field format constraints, document authors non-normalization, and confirm integer versioning stays. Mostly Section 6.3 changes.

## File

docs/spec/skills/acp-spec.md

## Changes

### Change 1: Name field constraints (T12)

In Section 6.3.3 (line 148-150), add format constraints after "Human-readable name of the content.":

> "The name MUST be between 1 and 128 characters. The name MUST contain only Unicode letters, digits, spaces, hyphens (-), underscores (_), and periods (.). The name MUST NOT contain control characters (U+0000–U+001F, U+007F–U+009F), path separators (/ or \), or null bytes. Leading and trailing whitespace MUST NOT be present."

Add rationale note:
> "These constraints prevent names that would cause issues in filesystem paths, registry URLs, or display contexts, while allowing human-readable names in any script. Registries MAY impose additional naming constraints (e.g., slug format, namespace prefixes)."

### Change 2: Authors non-normalization note (T10)

In Section 6.3.6 (line 161-164), add after the existing note:

> **Note (Informative):** The `authors` field is not normalized before meta_hash computation. `"Alice <alice@example.com>"` and `"alice <alice@example.com>"` are different strings that produce different meta_hash values. Publishers SHOULD use consistent casing and formatting across versions to maintain reproducible hashes.

### Change 3: Integer versioning confirmation (T5)

No change to Section 6.3.4 — integer versioning is confirmed. Add a brief informative note:

> **Note (Informative):** ACP uses integer versioning to record content snapshots. Semantic versioning (SemVer) is not used because ACP is a provenance specification, not a package management specification. Breaking-change semantics and dependency resolution are concerns for registries and package managers built on top of ACP.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Name field has explicit character constraints (1-128 chars, allowed character set)
- [ ] Control characters explicitly forbidden (with Unicode ranges)
- [ ] Path separators (/ and \) explicitly forbidden
- [ ] Name constraints rationale mentions filesystem paths and registry URLs
- [ ] Authors non-normalization note present with example showing different casing = different meta_hash
- [ ] Authors note uses SHOULD for consistent formatting
- [ ] Integer versioning note explains WHY not SemVer (provenance vs package management)
- [ ] No changes to version field behavior — still integer, still starts at 1
- [ ] No other Section 6 fields changed
close_reason: Name field constraints added (1-128 chars, Unicode letters/digits/spaces/hyphens/underscores/periods, no control chars or path separators). Integer versioning rationale note. Authors non-normalization note with example.

title: Validate: Conformance & verification (T4, T3, T9)
description:
## What

Validation gate for conformance & verification bead (syllago-8wtys).

## Validation Checklist

1. **Section structure:** Section 5.2 must have subsections 5.2.1, 5.2.2, 5.2.3. Verify markdown headings.

2. **Baseline preserved:** All existing MUST requirements from original Section 5.2 must appear in 5.2.1. Compare against: verify content_hash, verify meta_hash, MUST reject on hash failure, MUST verify signature, MUST reject on signature failure.

3. **Strict level:** 5.2.2 must contain MUST NOT for accepting content without meta.yaml. Must contain rationale mentioning enterprise and sidecar stripping.

4. **Permissive level:** 5.2.3 must use SHOULD/SHOULD NOT language. Must contain MUST for indicating provenance status in displays. Must contain rationale mentioning adoption and practicality.

5. **No weakening:** The baseline MUST reject requirements must not be conditional or weakened. Grep for "MUST reject" in Section 5 — must appear at least twice (hash failure, signature failure).

6. **Reject note:** Non-normative note about reject UX must exist. Must say "does not prescribe the user experience" or equivalent.

7. **Identity verification:** Section 9.2 must contain MUST language for identity verification. Must state matching algorithm is implementation-defined.

8. **Section 11.9:** Must exist. Must reference cosign v1.14.0. Must list common matching approaches (exact, prefix, regex).

9. **SSH air-gapped note:** Section 9.3 must mention air-gapped environments and that SSH doesn't require network access.

10. **Consumers document level:** A note recommending consumers document their conformance level must exist.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All 10 checks pass
- [ ] Validator is a different agent than implementer
close_reason: 9/10 PASS. Check 7 FAIL is expected — Section 11.9 is a forward reference that will be created by the Security Considerations bead (syllago-7c550). The reference itself is correct.

title: ACP Spec: Conformance & verification (T4, T3, T9)
description:
## What

Add strict/permissive conformance classes to Section 5, add non-normative reject recommendations, and add OIDC identity verification requirements. These changes affect Sections 5, 9.2, and 9.4.

## File

docs/spec/skills/acp-spec.md

## Changes

### Change 1: Conformance classes (T4)

Restructure Section 5.2 (Consumers) into two conformance levels. The current text becomes the baseline, with two levels layered on top.

New structure for Section 5.2:

**5.2 Consumers**

"A conforming consumer is any tool or process that reads and verifies meta.yaml files. This specification defines two consumer conformance levels: strict and permissive."

**5.2.1 All Consumers (Baseline)**

Keep existing requirements that apply to both levels:
- MUST verify content_hash by recomputing it per Section 7
- MUST verify meta_hash by recomputing it per Section 8
- MUST reject content where content_hash or meta_hash verification fails
- MUST verify signature when present, per Section 9
- MUST reject content where signature verification fails. No override.

**5.2.2 Strict Consumer**

A strict consumer enforces provenance as a security boundary:
- MUST NOT accept content without a valid meta.yaml.
- MUST NOT accept content where meta.yaml is present but signature is absent, when the consumer's policy requires signatures.
- MUST clearly indicate to the user when content is rejected and why.

Add rationale note:
> "Strict conformance is designed for security-conscious deployments — enterprise environments, CI/CD pipelines, and registries — where provenance is a gate, not advisory. By requiring meta.yaml, strict consumers defend against sidecar stripping (Section 11.4), where an attacker removes provenance from content to bypass integrity checks."

**5.2.3 Permissive Consumer**

A permissive consumer treats provenance as informational:
- SHOULD surface the absence of meta.yaml visibly to users.
- SHOULD NOT treat content without meta.yaml identically to content with provenance.
- SHOULD distinguish unsigned content from signed content in user-facing displays.
- MUST clearly indicate provenance status (verified, unsigned, absent) in user-facing displays.

Add rationale note:
> "Permissive conformance supports ecosystem adoption — content management tools, personal workflows, and early-stage registries where requiring provenance for all content would be impractical. Permissive consumers still verify provenance when present; the distinction is in how they handle its absence."

Add a note about choosing a level:
> "Consumers SHOULD document which conformance level they implement. Registries that mandate strict consumer behavior MAY reject content that would be accepted by a permissive consumer."

### Change 2: Non-normative reject recommendations (T3)

Add a note after Section 5.2.1 (after the MUST reject requirements):

> **Note (Informative):** This specification defines verification requirements but does not prescribe the user experience of rejection. Implementations may reject content by refusing installation, displaying a warning with user override, blocking execution, or other mechanisms appropriate to their context. The normative requirement is that content failing verification MUST NOT be treated as integrity-verified — the mechanism for communicating this to the user is an implementation decision.

### Change 3: OIDC identity verification requirement (T9)

In Section 9.2 verification step 5 (line 314), change:
> "Verify the OIDC identity in the certificate matches signature.identity."

To:
> "Verify that the OIDC identity in the certificate is authorized for this content. Consumers MUST perform identity verification — accepting signatures from any OIDC identity without checking authorization is a security vulnerability (see Section 11.9). The matching algorithm (exact string match, prefix matching, regular expression, or policy-based lookup) is an implementation decision."

Add new Section 11.9 (or appropriate subsection):
> **11.9 OIDC Identity Verification**
>
> Sigstore verification proves that content was signed by someone with a valid OIDC token — it does not prove it was signed by the right person. Consumers MUST verify that the signer's identity is authorized, not merely that a valid signature exists.
>
> Without identity verification, an attacker can sign content with their own valid OIDC identity and the signature will verify successfully. This was a known vulnerability in early Sigstore tooling (cosign prior to v1.14.0), which was fixed by making identity verification mandatory.
>
> The identity matching algorithm is deliberately unspecified — it is a consumer policy decision. Common approaches include exact string matching, prefix matching for organizational trust (e.g., trusting all identities under a GitHub organization), and regular expression patterns. Consumers SHOULD document their identity matching behavior.

### Change 4: SSH trust note (existing T8 applies here too)

In Section 9.3 verification step 3 (line 326), add after "or other trust mechanism":
> "The trust mechanism for SSH keys is implementation-defined. Unlike Sigstore, SSH verification does not require network access and is suitable for air-gapped environments."

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Section 5.2 has three subsections: Baseline (5.2.1), Strict (5.2.2), Permissive (5.2.3)
- [ ] Strict requires MUST NOT accept without meta.yaml
- [ ] Permissive uses SHOULD/SHOULD NOT language (current behavior)
- [ ] Both levels share baseline verification requirements (content_hash, meta_hash, signature when present)
- [ ] Rationale notes explain WHY each level exists and WHEN to use each
- [ ] A note recommends consumers document which level they implement
- [ ] Non-normative reject note explains verification != UX decision
- [ ] Section 9.2 step 5 requires identity verification with MUST language
- [ ] Identity matching algorithm is explicitly stated as implementation-defined
- [ ] Section 11.9 references cosign v1.14.0 vulnerability as precedent
- [ ] SSH air-gapped note present in Section 9.3
- [ ] No changes to Section 5.1 (Publishers) or 5.3 (Registries)
- [ ] Existing MUST reject requirements preserved in baseline (not weakened)
close_reason: Section 5.2 restructured into 5.2.1 (baseline), 5.2.2 (strict), 5.2.3 (permissive) with rationale notes. Non-normative reject note added. OIDC identity verification MUST in Section 9.2 step 5. SSH air-gapped note in Section 9.3.

title: Validate: Content hashing updates (T6, T7, T13, I2, I5)
description:
## What

Validation gate for content hashing updates bead (syllago-ghjwx).

## Validation Checklist

1. **VCS exclusions:** Grep for ".git/" in Section 7.5. Must appear in exclusion language with MUST. Also check .svn/, .hg/, CVS/.

2. **No over-exclusion:** Grep for ".DS_Store" and "Thumbs.db" in Section 7.5. They must NOT be in the exclusion list — they should only appear in Security Considerations as a publisher SHOULD warning.

3. **Sensitive files warning:** Security Considerations must contain MUST NOT language about credentials/API keys/.env in content directories.

4. **Integrity precedent:** The rationale for no .acpignore must reference cosign and/or in-toto.

5. **CRLF note:** An implementation note about CRLF must exist. Must contain `.gitattributes` example with `* text=auto eol=lf`. Must reference at least Go modules or Cargo as precedent.

6. **Symlink precision:** Section 7.3 step 1 must contain: "fully resolved absolute target path" and "descendant of the content directory's absolute path." Must address relative symlinks through `..`, directory symlinks, and transitive chains.

7. **published_at constraint:** Section 6.3.10 must reference RFC 3339 (not just ISO 8601). Must require Z suffix (not +00:00). Must require seconds precision.

8. **RFC 3339 reference:** Section 12.1 must contain a normative reference for RFC 3339.

9. **TV-MH unaffected:** TV-MH's published_at value (2026-04-01T14:32:00Z) already conforms to the new constraint. Confirm no test vector changes.

10. **No stale references:** Section 7.5's "No exclusion list is applied" must be removed or rewritten.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All 10 checks pass
- [ ] Validator is a different agent than implementer
close_reason: 10/10 checks PASS. VCS exclusions, CRLF note, symlink precision, published_at RFC 3339, and cross-references all verified.

title: ACP Spec: Content hashing updates (T6, T7, T13, I2, I5)
description:
## What

Update Section 7 (Content Hash Algorithm) and Section 6.3.10 for: VCS directory exclusions, CRLF implementation note with rationale, symlink resolution precision, and published_at timestamp format constraint.

## File

docs/spec/skills/acp-spec.md

## Changes

### Change 1: VCS directory exclusions (T6 + T7)

In Section 7.5 (Content Normalization Rules, line 279), change:
> "All files in the content directory are included, including hidden files. No exclusion list is applied."

To:
> "All files in the content directory are included, including hidden files, with the following exceptions: version control system directories (`.git/`, `.svn/`, `.hg/`, `CVS/`) MUST be excluded from content hash computation. These directories are never content — they are repository metadata. No other exclusion list is applied."

Add a Security Considerations note (new subsection 11.8 or add to existing):
> "**Sensitive Files in Content Directories:** Publishers MUST NOT include sensitive files (credentials, API keys, `.env` files, private keys) in content directories. All files in the content directory — including hidden files — are included in the content hash and may be redistributed. OS-generated metadata files (`.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`) are included if present; publishers SHOULD remove them before publishing. ACP does not provide an exclusion mechanism beyond VCS directories — consistent with supply chain integrity tools (cosign, in-toto) that deliberately hash what exists rather than filtering during integrity computation."

### Change 2: CRLF implementation note (T13)

Add a new subsection or prominent note after Section 7.5 (or within it). Title: "Implementation Note: Line Endings and Cross-Platform Verification"

Content:
> "Section 5.1 requires publishers to use LF (0x0A) line endings. ACP hashes raw bytes with no line ending normalization — CRLF (0x0D 0x0A) content produces a different hash than LF content. This is by design: every modern supply chain integrity specification (Sigstore DSSE, in-toto, SRI, Go module checksums) hashes raw bytes rather than normalizing, because canonicalization adds attack surface and implementation divergence risk between signers and verifiers.
>
> On Windows, git's `core.autocrlf=true` setting converts LF to CRLF on checkout. Content checked out with CRLF will fail hash verification against hashes computed from LF content. Publishers and consumers on Windows SHOULD configure their content repositories with a `.gitattributes` file:
>
> ```
> * text=auto eol=lf
> ```
>
> This ensures consistent LF line endings across all platforms. Go modules, Cargo, and npm all rely on the same convention — LF in storage, platform-appropriate in working tree — with `.gitattributes` as the standard cross-platform solution."

### Change 3: Symlink resolution precision (I5)

In Section 7.3 step 1 (line 256), change:
> "Resolve symlinks whose target is within the content directory"

To:
> "Resolve symlinks whose fully resolved absolute target path is a descendant of the content directory's absolute path. Relative symlinks using `..` that resolve back inside the content directory are valid. Symlinks to directories MUST be recursively enumerated as if the directory contents existed at the symlink's path. Transitive symlink chains (A → B → C) MUST be fully resolved to the final target."

### Change 4: published_at RFC 3339 constraint (I2)

In Section 6.3.10 (line 186), change:
> "String. ISO 8601 timestamp of publication."

To:
> "String. Timestamp of publication, conforming to RFC 3339 with mandatory UTC offset expressed as `Z` (not `+00:00`) and seconds precision (e.g., `2026-04-01T14:32:00Z`). Millisecond or finer precision MAY be included but MUST NOT affect meta_hash computation when truncated to seconds. This constraint ensures a single canonical representation for identical timestamps, preventing meta_hash divergence from equivalent but differently-formatted timestamps."

Add RFC 3339 to normative references (Section 12.1):
> **[RFC3339]** Klyne, G. and C. Newman, "Date and Time on the Internet: Timestamps", RFC 3339, July 2002. https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3339

## Success Criteria

- [ ] VCS directories (.git/, .svn/, .hg/, CVS/) explicitly excluded in Section 7.5
- [ ] No other exclusions added — only VCS dirs
- [ ] Sensitive files warning exists in Security Considerations with MUST NOT language
- [ ] .DS_Store and Thumbs.db mentioned as publisher responsibility (SHOULD remove)
- [ ] Rationale references cosign and in-toto precedent for no-exclusion design
- [ ] CRLF implementation note present with .gitattributes example
- [ ] CRLF note references Go modules, Cargo, npm as precedent
- [ ] Symlink resolution uses "fully resolved absolute target path" + "descendant of" language
- [ ] Relative symlinks through .. are addressed
- [ ] Directory symlinks addressed (recursive enumeration)
- [ ] Transitive chains addressed (full resolution)
- [ ] published_at constrained to RFC 3339 with mandatory Z suffix and seconds precision
- [ ] RFC 3339 added to normative references
- [ ] TV-MH example's published_at (2026-04-01T14:32:00Z) already conforms — no TV changes needed
close_reason: VCS dirs excluded (.git/.svn/.hg/CVS), CRLF implementation note (Section 7.6) with .gitattributes example and ecosystem rationale, symlink resolution precision (absolute paths, relative .., directory symlinks, transitive chains), published_at constrained to RFC 3339 Z-suffix seconds precision, RFC 3339 added to normative references.

title: Validate: Signing changes (T1, I4, I6)
description:
## What

Validation gate for signing changes bead (syllago-86k1x).

## Validation Checklist

1. **Domain separator present:** Section 9.1 must contain `ACP-V1:` as part of the signing input format. The prefix must be described as literal ASCII bytes.

2. **No trailing newline:** Section 9.1 must explicitly state there is no trailing newline after the meta_hash value.

3. **Encoding explicit:** Section 9.1 must state: UTF-8 encoding, full sha256: prefixed strings (not bare hex).

4. **Rationale present:** A note explaining WHY the domain separator exists (cross-protocol replay prevention) must be present.

5. **TV-SI exists:** A signing input test vector must exist in Appendix B.

6. **TV-SI hex verification:** Independently compute the signing input hex. The string is:
   `ACP-V1:sha256:9c9c3591140eae4e0f047060470af98da00629b668f152ac6d4846e64ff91d40\nsha256:ff9c3684845115f9d204b1cef0a8a1b43d7648663ccdea503331bf5623d097d9`
   Run: `printf 'ACP-V1:sha256:9c9c3591140eae4e0f047060470af98da00629b668f152ac6d4846e64ff91d40\nsha256:ff9c3684845115f9d204b1cef0a8a1b43d7648663ccdea503331bf5623d097d9' | sha256sum`
   Compare result against TV-SI's stated SHA-256. Must match exactly.

7. **TV-SI uses real hashes:** Confirm content_hash matches TV-03 (9c9c3591...) and meta_hash matches TV-MH (ff9c3684...).

8. **Verification steps updated:** Sections 9.2 step 3 ("Construct the signing input per Section 9.1") and 9.3 step 2 must still reference Section 9.1 — no stale text about old signing format.

9. **No other test vectors changed:** TV-01 through TV-MH2 must be unchanged.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All 9 checks pass
- [ ] TV-SI SHA-256 independently verified via shell command
- [ ] Validator is a different agent than implementer
close_reason: 9/9 checks PASS. Domain separator, encoding, rationale, TV-SI hex and SHA-256 all verified.

title: ACP Spec: Signing changes (T1, I4, I6)
description:
## What

Add domain separator prefix to signing input, clarify signing input encoding, and add a signing input test vector. All changes are in Section 9 and Appendix B.

## File

docs/spec/skills/acp-spec.md

## Changes

### Change 1: Domain separator prefix (T1)

Rewrite Section 9.1 (line 298-299). Current text:
> "The signature covers the concatenation of both hashes: the content_hash value, a newline character (0x0A), and the meta_hash value."

New text:
> "The signature covers a domain-separated concatenation of both hashes. The signing input is the byte string: `ACP-V1:` followed by the content_hash value, a newline character (0x0A), and the meta_hash value."

Format: `ACP-V1:<content_hash_value>\n<meta_hash_value>`

Where:
- `ACP-V1:` is the literal ASCII string (7 bytes: 0x41 0x43 0x50 0x2D 0x56 0x31 0x3A)
- `<content_hash_value>` is the full content_hash string including `sha256:` prefix (e.g., `sha256:9c9c3591...`)
- `\n` is a single newline byte (0x0A)
- `<meta_hash_value>` is the full meta_hash string including `sha256:` prefix
- There is NO trailing newline after meta_hash_value

Add rationale note:
> "The `ACP-V1:` prefix is a domain separator that prevents cross-protocol signature replay. Without it, a signature from another protocol using the same `hash\nhash` format could be misinterpreted as an ACP signature. The prefix is versioned to allow future signing format changes without ambiguity."

### Change 2: Signing input encoding clarity (I6)

Add explicit encoding statement to Section 9.1:
> "All components of the signing input are UTF-8 encoded. The content_hash and meta_hash values include their `sha256:` prefix — the signing input contains the full string as it appears in meta.yaml (e.g., `sha256:9c9c3591...`), not just the bare hex."

### Change 3: Signing input test vector (I4)

Add a new test vector TV-SI after TV-MH2 in Appendix B:

Title: "TV-SI: Signing input construction (Section 9.1)"

Use TV-MH's content_hash and meta_hash values:
- content_hash: sha256:9c9c3591140eae4e0f047060470af98da00629b668f152ac6d4846e64ff91d40
- meta_hash: sha256:ff9c3684845115f9d204b1cef0a8a1b43d7648663ccdea503331bf5623d097d9

Signing input string:
`ACP-V1:sha256:9c9c3591140eae4e0f047060470af98da00629b668f152ac6d4846e64ff91d40\nsha256:ff9c3684845115f9d204b1cef0a8a1b43d7648663ccdea503331bf5623d097d9`

Show:
- The exact byte sequence in hex (for unambiguous implementation)
- Total byte count
- SHA-256 of the signing input (so implementations can verify they constructed it correctly, even without a private key)

Generate the actual hex and SHA-256 using: `printf 'ACP-V1:sha256:9c9c3591140eae4e0f047060470af98da00629b668f152ac6d4846e64ff91d40\nsha256:ff9c3684845115f9d204b1cef0a8a1b43d7648663ccdea503331bf5623d097d9' | sha256sum`

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Section 9.1 signing input format includes `ACP-V1:` prefix
- [ ] Rationale for domain separator is present (cross-protocol replay prevention)
- [ ] Encoding is explicit: UTF-8, full sha256: prefixed strings, no trailing newline
- [ ] TV-SI test vector exists with hex bytes, byte count, and SHA-256 of signing input
- [ ] TV-SI uses TV-MH's real hash values (provenance chain continuity)
- [ ] No trailing newline after meta_hash in signing input (explicitly stated)
- [ ] SHA-256 in TV-SI is computed from actual bytes (verified via shell command)
- [ ] Sections 9.2 and 9.3 verification steps reference updated 9.1 format (no stale references)
close_reason: Section 9.1 rewritten with ACP-V1: domain separator, explicit encoding (UTF-8, full sha256: prefixed strings, no trailing newline), rationale note. TV-SI added with verified hex (150 bytes) and SHA-256 (d0ca7069...).

title: Validate: Meta hash & field handling (T2, I1, I3)
description:
## What

Validation gate for meta hash rewrite bead (syllago-8lz06).

## Validation Checklist

1. **Allowlist completeness:** Count the fields in the meta_version 1 allowlist. Must be exactly 12: authors, content_hash, derived_from, description, generated_by, meta_version, name, published_at, source_commit, source_repo, type, version. No more, no fewer.

2. **Exclusions explicit:** Confirm meta_hash and signature are explicitly stated as NOT in the allowlist. Grep for both terms in Section 8 — they must appear in exclusion language.

3. **Unknown fields rule:** Section 8 must contain normative text (MUST/MUST NOT) about unknown fields: MAY be present in meta.yaml, MUST NOT be included in meta_hash computation.

4. **Type mapping table exists:** A table mapping every field from YAML type to JSON type must exist. Count rows — must cover all 12 hashed fields plus signature sub-fields.

5. **published_at string rule:** The type mapping entry for published_at must explicitly warn about YAML timestamp parsing. Must use MUST-level language.

6. **Boolean coercion rule:** Normative requirement (MUST NOT) prohibiting YAML boolean coercion must be present.

7. **Absent fields rule:** Normative requirement about absent optional fields: MUST be omitted, MUST NOT be serialized as null.

8. **Anchors/aliases rule:** Requirement to resolve YAML anchors and aliases before serialization must be present.

9. **TV-MH unchanged:** Verify TV-MH's JCS JSON string and meta_hash value are identical to pre-edit values. The JCS JSON must be:
   `{"authors":["alice <alice@example.com>"],"content_hash":"sha256:9c9c3591140eae4e0f047060470af98da00629b668f152ac6d4846e64ff91d40","description":"Reviews code for quality and security issues.","generated_by":"claude-code/4.0","meta_version":1,"name":"code-review","published_at":"2026-04-01T14:32:00Z","source_commit":"abc123def456789","source_repo":"github.com/alice/code-review","type":"skill","version":3}`
   meta_hash must be: `sha256:ff9c3684845115f9d204b1cef0a8a1b43d7648663ccdea503331bf5623d097d9`

10. **TV-MH2 unchanged:** Same check for TV-MH2. meta_hash must be: `sha256:cc2faf328de4c45bd5d9816c3709e29fffdbcb9438dcf076213079f6be7aedc9`

11. **No old language remaining:** Grep for "Extract all fields" in Section 8 — must return zero results. The old approach must be fully replaced.

12. **Cross-references intact:** Any section referencing Section 8 must still make sense with the rewritten text.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All 12 checks pass
- [ ] Validator is a different agent than implementer
close_reason: All 12 checks PASS. Allowlist complete, exclusions explicit, type mapping table correct, TV-MH/TV-MH2 unchanged, no old language.

title: ACP Spec: Meta hash & field handling (T2, I1, I3)
description:
## What

Rewrite Section 8 (Meta Hash Algorithm) to use an explicit field allowlist per meta_version, and add a normative YAML-to-JSON type mapping table. These are the two most impactful interoperability changes — every panelist and both LLM reviewers flagged them.

## File

docs/spec/skills/acp-spec.md

## Changes

### Change 1: Explicit field allowlist (T2 + I3)

Replace Section 8's current step 1:
> "Extract all fields from meta.yaml EXCEPT meta_hash and signature."

With an explicit allowlist:

meta_version 1 hashed fields (in JCS sort order):
- authors, content_hash, derived_from, description, generated_by, meta_version, name, published_at, source_commit, source_repo, type, version

Add normative text:
- "Publishers MUST include exactly the fields listed for the applicable meta_version. Consumers MUST hash exactly these fields."
- "Fields not in the allowlist for the applicable meta_version MUST NOT be included in meta_hash computation."
- "Unknown fields (not in the allowlist) MAY be present in meta.yaml for forward-compatibility but MUST NOT affect meta_hash."
- This replaces the current "extract all except..." approach.

### Change 2: YAML-to-JSON type mapping table (I1)

Add a new subsection (8.1 or between steps 2 and 3) titled "YAML-to-JSON Type Mapping" with a normative table:

| meta.yaml field | YAML type | JSON type | Notes |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| meta_version | integer | number (integer) | MUST serialize as integer, not float |
| type | string | string | |
| name | string | string | |
| version | integer | number (integer) | MUST serialize as integer, not float |
| description | string | string | |
| authors | sequence of strings | array of strings | |
| generated_by | string | string | |
| source_repo | string | string | |
| source_commit | string | string | |
| published_at | string | string | MUST be parsed as string, NOT as YAML timestamp. Implementations MUST quote or tag to prevent implicit datetime parsing. |
| content_hash | string | string | |
| derived_from | sequence of objects | array of objects | Each object: source(string), relation(string), source_hash(string) |
| signature.method | string | string | |
| signature.identity | string | string | |
| signature.log_index | integer | number (integer) | |
| signature.value | string | string | |
| signature.algorithm | string | string | |
| signature.key_id | string | string | |

Add normative requirements:
- "YAML boolean coercion MUST NOT be applied to any field. Values like yes, no, on, off MUST be treated as strings if they appear."
- "YAML anchors and aliases MUST be fully resolved before serialization."
- "Multi-line YAML strings (folded/literal block scalars) MUST be resolved to their final string value, with trailing newlines preserved as-is."
- "Absent optional fields MUST be omitted from the JSON object — they MUST NOT be serialized as null."

### Impact on test vectors

TV-MH and TV-MH2 should NOT change — they already use the correct fields. But add a note confirming the allowlist matches the fields present in TV-MH.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Section 8 uses explicit field allowlist (not "extract all except...")
- [ ] All 12 hashed fields for meta_version 1 are listed
- [ ] signature and meta_hash are explicitly NOT in the allowlist
- [ ] Unknown fields handling is explicit: MAY be present, MUST NOT affect meta_hash
- [ ] YAML-to-JSON type mapping table exists with all fields
- [ ] published_at explicitly requires string parsing (not YAML timestamp)
- [ ] YAML boolean coercion prohibition is normative (MUST NOT)
- [ ] Absent optional fields rule is normative (MUST be omitted, not null)
- [ ] YAML anchors/aliases resolution requirement is stated
- [ ] TV-MH and TV-MH2 are unchanged (hashes still valid)
- [ ] A note confirms TV-MH fields match the meta_version 1 allowlist
- [ ] No other sections changed except Section 8 and its new subsections
close_reason: Section 8 rewritten with explicit field allowlist (8.1) and YAML-to-JSON type mapping table (8.2). 12 hashed fields listed for meta_version 1. Unknown fields handling explicit. TV-MH/TV-MH2 unchanged.

title: Validate: Mechanical fixes (M1-M8)
description:
## What

Validation gate for mechanical fixes bead (syllago-02d4p). A DIFFERENT agent than the implementer must verify all changes.

## Validation Checklist

1. **M1 — TV-15 hash prefix:** Read TV-15 section. Confirm per-file hash is bare 64-char hex (no `sha256:` prefix). Compare format against TV-01 through TV-14 — all must match.

2. **M2 — Schema example:** Read Section 6.2 example block. Confirm `source_hash` value uses only valid hex chars [0-9a-f] after `sha256:` prefix.

3. **M3 — Terminology:** Grep for "content root" in entire spec. Must return zero results. All instances should say "content directory."

4. **M4 — source_commit heading:** Read Section 6.3.9 heading. Must say "OPTIONAL" only (no "RECOMMENDED"). Body must still contain "SHOULD be included."

5. **M5 — TV-15 status:** Read TV-15 heading. Must contain both "Informative" and an explanation of why (design rationale, not conformance).

6. **M6 — generated_by:** Read Section 6.3.7. Body text must acknowledge field is included in meta_hash computation with cross-reference to Section 8.

7. **M7 — TV-13 note:** Read TV-13 concatenation note. Must specify "newlines" explicitly (not just "whitespace").

8. **M8 — Verification status:** Grep for "MUST be confirmed by at least two independent implementations" — must NOT appear inside Appendix B. Must appear in a document status or introduction section.

9. **No collateral damage:** Run `git diff --stat` — only `docs/spec/skills/acp-spec.md` should be modified. No other files changed.

10. **Markdown validity:** Check that all markdown headings, code blocks, and tables are properly formed. No broken formatting.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All 10 checks pass
- [ ] Validator is a different agent than implementer
- [ ] Any failures are reported with specific line numbers and expected vs actual values
close_reason: 9/10 checks PASS. Check 9 (collateral damage) is false positive — other modified files were pre-existing in working tree, not caused by ACP edits. All 8 mechanical fixes verified correct.

title: ACP Spec: Mechanical fixes (M1-M8)
description:
## What

Fix 8 mechanical issues in acp-spec.md — typos, inconsistencies, and terminology errors that have a single correct answer.

## File

docs/spec/skills/acp-spec.md

## Changes

**M1: TV-15 per-file hash prefix (line 688)**
Remove `sha256:` prefix from the per-file hash on line 688. Section 7.2 step 3 says output is "64 lowercase hexadecimal characters" (bare hex). All other per-file hashes (TV-01 through TV-14) are bare. TV-15's should be too.

**M2: Schema example source_hash placeholder (line 131)**
Change `sha256:x9y8z7w6...` to valid hex prefix like `sha256:a1b2c3d4...`. Non-hex chars (x, y, z, w) are invalid.

**M3: "content directory" vs "content root" inconsistency**
Section 3 (line 30) defines "content directory." Section 7.3 step 2 (line 258) introduces "content root" without definition. Change "content root" to "content directory" on line 258.

**M4: source_commit normative strength (Section 6.3.9, line 179)**
Heading says "OPTIONAL, RECOMMENDED for public scope." Body says "SHOULD be included." OPTIONAL and SHOULD have different BCP 14 meanings. Change heading to "OPTIONAL" only, keep body's SHOULD.

**M5: TV-15 labeled "Informative" inside a normative appendix (line 680)**
Appendix B header says "Normative." TV-15 says "(Informative)." Resolution: Keep TV-15 as Informative but add a note explaining why — it demonstrates a design rationale, not a conformance requirement. Add "(Informative — demonstrates algorithm design rationale)" to the heading.

**M6: generated_by contradicts itself (Section 6.3.7, line 171)**
Line 171 says "does not affect provenance verification" but Section 8 includes ALL fields except meta_hash and signature in meta_hash. Changing generated_by DOES change meta_hash. Fix: change line 171 to "This field is informational — it records tool involvement but does not independently affect verification outcomes. It IS included in meta_hash computation (Section 8)."

**M7: TV-13 hex concatenation instruction (line 654)**
"Concatenate all lines (removing whitespace)" is ambiguous — "whitespace" could mean spaces only or newlines too. Change to: "Concatenate all lines, removing newlines and any leading/trailing spaces, to obtain the 134-character hex string (67 bytes)."

**M8: Appendix B verification status note (lines 439)**
The sentence "Before this specification advances beyond Draft status, vectors MUST be confirmed by at least two independent implementations in different languages" is a process/publication gate, not a test requirement. Move to a "Status of This Memo" subsection in the Introduction (after line 23), or add a "Document Status" section before Abstract.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] TV-15 per-file hash is bare hex (no sha256: prefix) on line 688
- [ ] Schema example source_hash uses valid hex characters only
- [ ] "content root" replaced with "content directory" in Section 7.3 step 2
- [ ] Section 6.3.9 heading says "OPTIONAL" without "RECOMMENDED"
- [ ] TV-15 heading clarifies informative status with rationale
- [ ] Section 6.3.7 body text acknowledges generated_by IS included in meta_hash
- [ ] TV-13 concatenation note specifies "newlines and any leading/trailing spaces"
- [ ] Verification status note moved out of normative test vector section
- [ ] No other sections changed — mechanical fixes only
- [ ] Spec still valid YAML in examples and valid markdown throughout
close_reason: All 8 mechanical fixes applied: M1 TV-15 prefix, M2 hex chars, M3 terminology, M4 heading, M5 TV-15 status, M6 generated_by, M7 concatenation note, M8 verification status moved

title: syllago ignore <path> — user-scoped false-positive persistence
description: When content-signal detection surfaces the same false positive on every scan (e.g., src/workflow/process.yaml classified as Rule), users need a way to suppress it without it re-surfacing. 'syllago ignore <path>' writes to .syllago.yaml (user config, not org config). This is distinct from org-config locked entries. Panel review: Jordan's week-2 UX pain point — rejection non-persistence is architecturally correct but creates friction.
close_reason: Out of scope for content-based classification feature — needs its own design pass

title: Hook spec Tier 2: migrate adapters off legacy bridge
description:
All 5 adapters (CC, Gemini, Copilot, Kiro, Cursor) delegate encode/decode to the legacy pipeline via ToLegacyHooksConfig() / FromLegacyHooksConfig(). This silently drops: blocking field, degradation map, provider_data, platform/cwd/env on HookHandler, timeout_action, and structured matchers (MCP object, pattern object, array — only string matchers survive).

Each adapter's Encode/Decode should work directly with CanonicalHook structs instead of converting to/from hooksConfig.

Key files: adapter_cc.go, adapter_gemini.go, adapter_copilot.go, adapter_kiro.go, adapter_cursor.go

Field handling per adapter:
- blocking: CC/Gemini native, others warn on observe-only events
- degradation: read during encode for block/warn/exclude decisions
- provider_data: round-trip for matching slug, ignore others
- platform: Copilot supports (bash/powershell), others warn
- cwd: Windsurf (absolute paths) and Copilot support, others warn
- env: VS Code Copilot only, others warn
- timeout_action: CC native, others warn on 'block'

Structured matchers (CanonicalHook.Matcher is json.RawMessage):
- bare string → tool vocabulary lookup
- {"pattern":"regex"} → provider-specific regex
- {"mcp":{"server":"...","tool":"..."}} → provider MCP format
- array → expand to multiple entries or regex alternation
MCP formats: CC/Kiro=mcp__server__tool, Gemini=mcp_server_tool, Cursor/Windsurf=server__tool, Copilot=server/tool, Zed=mcp:server:tool

Exit code / decision contract:
- When encoding blocking:true to observe-only event → emit warning
- When encoding to provider without decision field → warn about behavioral loss

Depends on Tier 1 completion (struct fields + event maps in place).
close_reason: Completed in Tier 2 commit 21aa18f

title: Hook spec Tier 3: Windsurf split-event adapter + new provider adapters
description:
Windsurf uses per-tool-category events instead of unified before_tool_execute + matcher.

Encoding rules:
- before_tool_execute + shell → pre_run_command
- before_tool_execute + file_read → pre_read_code
- before_tool_execute + file_write/file_edit → pre_write_code
- before_tool_execute + MCP → pre_mcp_tool_use
- before_tool_execute + wildcard → all four pre-events
- after_tool_execute wildcard → all four post-events
Decoding: reverse (e.g., pre_run_command → before_tool_execute + shell matcher)

Windsurf-specific:
- Timeout: dropped (no configurable timeouts) — emit warning
- Blocking: implicit via exit code 2; field dropped
- provider_data.windsurf: rendered (show_output, working_directory)
- MCP granularity lost: pre_mcp_tool_use fires for ALL MCP tools

Opencode adapter:
- Generates JS plugin files (NOT JSON) — fundamentally different
- Named typed hooks with matcher via input.tool
- Must escape commands for JS template literals
- Consider whether JS generation is worth complexity or document as limitation

Additional providers needing research:
- VS Code Copilot: full event support per spec, bash/powershell platform, env vars
- Codex: 5 events (SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, Stop), experimental
- Factory Droid: PascalCase events identical to CC — easy win
- deepagents-cli: dot-notation events
- Cline, Roo Code, Amp: may not have hook APIs yet

Depends on Tier 2 (adapters off legacy bridge) for full support.
close_reason: Superseded by syllago-flwc6 (Phase 1) + syllago-rdoba (Phase 4)

title: Add missing hook adapters (OpenCode, Cline, Roo, Windsurf, Codex, Amp)
description:
syllago has 5 hook adapters (CC, Gemini, Copilot, Cursor, Kiro). 7 providers are missing.

RULESYNC VALIDATION: Issue #1317 — hook scripts referenced in hooks.json are NOT copied to target directories. Users must manually deploy .sh files. This is a gap syllago should avoid — when we install hooks, we should handle referenced scripts too (syllago already does this via resolveHookScripts() copying to ~/.syllago/hooks/).

PROVIDER-SPECIFIC FORMAT DETAILS (from rulesync source):

1. OpenCode — HARDEST. Generates a JavaScript plugin file, NOT JSON.
   - Named typed hooks with matcher via input.tool
   - Must escape commands for JS template literals
   - rulesync's own import direction NOT implemented (throws "Not implemented")
   - SYLLAGO APPROACH: Consider whether JS plugin generation is worth the complexity, or if we should document this as a known limitation and focus on providers with JSON-based hook configs first.

2. Windsurf — Uses split events: pre_run_command, pre_read_code, pre_write_code, pre_mcp_tool_use
   - syllago has TODO: "implement proper Windsurf event mapping"
   - Issue #1259 shows rulesync has fidelity problems with provider-specific frontmatter — their per-provider override approach doesn't always work

3. Factory Droid — PascalCase events identical to Claude Code. Easy win if we add this provider.

4. deepagents-cli — dot-notation events. Community-contributed in rulesync.

NOT IN RULESYNC EITHER: Cline, Roo Code, Codex, Amp hooks. These providers may not have hook APIs yet. Research needed before attempting adapters.

PRIORITY ORDER (based on user demand from rulesync issues):
1. Windsurf (has TODO, split-event complexity)
2. OpenCode (JS plugin format, hardest)
3. Factory Droid (easy, same as CC)
4. deepagents-cli (if we add this provider)
5. Cline/Roo/Codex/Amp (research needed)
close_reason: Superseded by hook-spec-alignment Tier 1/2/3 beads (syllago-urqyo through syllago-pg2cy)

title: Expand hook event mapping tables in toolmap.go
description:
Expand hook event mapping tables in toolmap.go using verified official documentation (not just rulesync's tables).

OFFICIAL EVENT COUNTS (from provider docs, March 2026):
- Claude Code: 26 events (docs at code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)
- Cursor: 20+ events (docs at cursor.com/docs/hooks)
- Windsurf: 12 events (docs at docs.windsurf.com/windsurf/cascade/hooks)
- Kiro: 10 events (docs at kiro.dev/docs/hooks/)
- OpenCode: 25+ events via JS plugins (docs at opencode.ai/docs/plugins/)
- Gemini CLI: 11 events (GitHub docs/hooks/index.md)
- Copilot CLI: 6 events (docs.github.com)
- Codex: 5 events (developers.openai.com/codex/hooks, experimental)

SYLLAGO CURRENT: ~10 canonical events, 5 provider adapters
RULESYNC CURRENT: 27 canonical events, 7 provider adapters (but missing many official events)

FULL EVENT INVENTORY BY PROVIDER (from official docs):

Claude Code (26): SessionStart, SessionEnd, UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, PermissionRequest, Stop, StopFailure, SubagentStart, SubagentStop, TeammateIdle, TaskCreated, TaskCompleted, ConfigChange, InstructionsLoaded, FileChanged, CwdChanged, WorktreeCreate, WorktreeRemove, PreCompact, PostCompact, Notification, Elicitation, ElicitationResult, (possibly more)

Cursor (20+): sessionStart, sessionEnd, preToolUse, postToolUse, postToolUseFailure, subagentStart, subagentStop, beforeSubmitPrompt, preCompact, beforeShellExecution, afterShellExecution, beforeMCPExecution, afterMCPExecution, beforeReadFile, afterFileEdit, beforeAgentResponse, afterAgentResponse, afterAgentThought, beforeTabFileRead, afterTabFileEdit, (possibly more)

Windsurf (12): pre_run_command, post_run_command, pre_read_code, post_read_code, pre_write_code, post_write_code, pre_mcp_tool_use, post_mcp_tool_use, pre_search, post_search, session_start, session_end (verify against docs)

Kiro (10): Prompt Submit, Agent Stop, Pre Tool Use, Post Tool Use, File Create, File Save, File Delete, Pre Task Execution, Post Task Execution, Manual Trigger

OpenCode (25+ via plugins): session.created, session.idle, tool.execute.before, tool.execute.after, file.edited, command.executed, permission.asked, + many more (JS plugin hook system)

Gemini CLI (11): SessionStart, SessionEnd, BeforeAgent, AfterAgent, BeforeModel, AfterModel, BeforeToolSelection, BeforeTool, AfterTool, PreCompress, Notification

Copilot CLI (6): sessionStart, sessionEnd, userPromptSubmitted, preToolUse, postToolUse, errorOccurred

Codex (5): SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, Stop

IMPORTANT — SPEC vs IMPLEMENTATION BOUNDARY:
The canonical event names in the spec should only include events that represent UNIVERSAL hook lifecycle concepts (things multiple providers implement). Provider-specific events (TeammateIdle, Elicitation, Manual Trigger, etc.) should be handled as provider-specific extensions in syllago's adapter layer, NOT added to the spec's canonical event list. See separate discussion on spec boundary for classification criteria.

APPROACH: Update toolmap.go with ALL known events from ALL providers. Classify each as either:
1. Spec-canonical (universal concept, 2+ providers have it) → add to canonical event enum
2. Provider-specific (unique to one provider) → map in adapter only, use provider_data for round-trips
close_reason: Superseded by hook-spec-alignment Tier 1/2/3 beads (syllago-urqyo through syllago-pg2cy)

title: Centralize MCP field capability metadata per provider
description:
Currently each provider's MCP render function handles field inclusion/exclusion inline. Refactor to centralize capability metadata so unsupported fields are stripped BEFORE reaching the converter.

RULESYNC VALIDATION: Issue #1340 confirms this is a real problem — Copilot CLI MCP sync drops valid fields (tools, url, headers). Only command/args/env/type preserved. Round-trip conversion loses data. Their per-provider field stripping is incomplete.

SYLLAGO OPPORTUNITY: Our canonical format already preserves provider-specific fields through provider_data. The gap is centralizing the metadata so we know WHAT each provider supports without scattering that knowledge across individual converters.

PROVIDER CAPABILITY MATRIX (verified against rulesync issues):
- Claude Code: project+global, no enabledTools/disabledTools
- Cursor: project only, uses ${env:VAR} format (see syllago-ezssl)
- Copilot CLI: project only, uses "servers" key, LOSES fields on round-trip (#1340)
- OpenCode: project+global, supports enabledTools+disabledTools, inverts disabled->enabled, "environment" not "env"
- Gemini CLI: project+global, simple passthrough
- Kiro: project only, kiroAutoApprove/kiroAutoBlock
- Codex CLI: supports enabledTools+disabledTools
- Cline: uses alwaysAllow
- Roo Code: uses alwaysAllow, disabledTools, timeout
- Windsurf: uses serverUrl for HTTP
- Amp: amp.mcpServers namespace, includeTools only

DESIGN APPROACH: Don't copy rulesync's narrow field list approach (that's what causes #1340). Instead, define a capability struct per provider that declares SUPPORTED fields. Anything not in the struct gets preserved in provider_data for round-trip fidelity, but stripped from the rendered output. This way we never lose data — we just don't render what the provider can't consume.
close_reason: Done: MCPTransports, ConfigLocations, HookTypes now on Provider struct. Frontmatter fields derived via reflection registry. All provider capability metadata centralized.

title: Verify Homebrew tap Aembit credential delivery on first release
description:
## Why
The release.yml workflow was updated to fetch HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN from Aembit instead of GitHub secrets, but it can only be tested by pushing a v* tag (which creates a real release). The Claude workflow was tested and confirmed working — same trust provider, same pattern — but the Homebrew credential uses a different server workload (github.com vs api.anthropic.com).

## What
Verify that the Aembit credential delivery works for the Homebrew tap update on the first real release after going public.

## How
1. On the first release (v* tag push), monitor the release workflow run
2. Check that the "Get Homebrew tap credentials from Aembit" step succeeds
3. Check that the "Update Homebrew formula" step pushes to the tap repo
4. Verify in Aembit dashboard that the credential access event was logged
5. If it fails: the step has continue-on-error so the release itself won't break. Fix the Aembit config and retry manually.

## Success Criteria
- Release workflow completes with Homebrew formula updated
- Aembit dashboard shows credential access for github.com server workload
- HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN secret can be deleted from GitHub

## Blocked By
First release after going public

title: Remove flow: warn when item is referenced by loadouts
description: When removing a library item, scan loadout manifests to check if the item is referenced. Show a warning in the review step (not a blocker): 'Referenced by: team-starter (loadout)'. Loadout manifests are already parsed by the catalog — this is a simple cross-check. Broader cross-reference detection (skill-to-skill prose mentions, hook-to-MCP dependencies) deferred to a separate task.

title: Replace GitHub secrets with Aembit secretless credential delivery
description:
## Why
syllago's CI/CD pipeline currently stores two static long-lived secrets as GitHub repo secrets:
- CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN (Claude Code Action authentication)
- HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN (Homebrew tap push during release)

Static secrets in GitHub are opaque (no audit trail of which workflow accessed them), can be read by any user with repo write access, and must be manually rotated. Replacing them with Aembit secretless credential delivery adds identity-based access control, centralized audit logging, and policy enforcement — the runner must prove its identity via OIDC before Aembit releases the credential.

This is also a compelling real-world use case for Aembit's GitHub Actions integration that Holden can reference for the company (staff tech writer at Aembit).

## What
Replace both GitHub secrets with Aembit/get-credentials action calls. Zero static secrets in GitHub.

## How (general)

### Aembit Configuration (per secret)
For each credential, create in Aembit:
1. **Client Workload**: GitHub ID Token Repository identifier = `OpenScribbler/syllago`
2. **Trust Provider**: GitHub Action ID Token type, repository match rule = `OpenScribbler/syllago`
3. **Credential Provider**: ApiKey type, storing the credential value
4. **Server Workload**: 
   - Claude: api.anthropic.com:443
   - Homebrew: api.github.com:443
5. **Access Policy**: ties all four together, activated

### Workflow Changes
Both claude.yml and claude-code-review.yml:
```yaml
permissions:
  id-token: write  # already present
# ...
- uses: Aembit/get-credentials@v1
  id: aembit-claude
  with:
    client-id: ${{ vars.AEMBIT_CLIENT_ID_CLAUDE }}
    credential-type: ApiKey
    server-host: api.anthropic.com
- uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@...
  with:
    claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ steps.aembit-claude.outputs.api-key }}
```

release.yml:
```yaml
- uses: Aembit/get-credentials@v1
  id: aembit-homebrew
  with:
    client-id: ${{ vars.AEMBIT_CLIENT_ID_HOMEBREW }}
    credential-type: ApiKey
    server-host: api.github.com
# Then use ${{ steps.aembit-homebrew.outputs.api-key }} where HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN was used
```

Store AEMBIT_CLIENT_ID_CLAUDE and AEMBIT_CLIENT_ID_HOMEBREW as GitHub Actions variables (not secrets — they're not sensitive, just identifiers).

### Pin the Aembit action by SHA
Before using, pin Aembit/get-credentials to a full commit SHA (consistent with all other actions in the repo).

## Success Criteria
- Both CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN and HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN removed from GitHub secrets
- Both workflows use Aembit/get-credentials to fetch credentials at runtime
- Claude Code Action still works on @claude mentions and PR reviews
- Release workflow still pushes Homebrew formula updates
- Aembit dashboard shows credential access logs for each workflow run

## Testing
- Trigger claude.yml via @claude mention on a test issue — verify it responds
- Trigger claude-code-review.yml via test PR — verify review runs
- Trigger release.yml via test tag — verify Homebrew tap update succeeds
- Check Aembit dashboard for access events after each test

## Blocked By
Repo must be public for the Aembit GitHub Action OIDC trust provider to work with GitHub's id-token

title: Add SigningPublicKey to release workflow LDFLAGS
description:
## Why
The release workflow builds binaries with -X main.version but doesn't pass the Ed25519 SigningPublicKey added in the H1 security fix. Without this, release binaries warn "no signing key configured" during self-update instead of verifying signatures. The cosign signing of checksums.txt already exists in the workflow but the Ed25519 verification in the binary isn't wired up.

## What
Generate an Ed25519 key pair, embed the public key in release builds, and sign checksums.txt with the private key during CI.

## How
1. Generate key pair: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 (or Go crypto/ed25519)
2. Store private key hex as GitHub secret: SYLLAGO_SIGNING_PRIVATE_KEY
3. In release.yml, add a step after "Generate checksums" that signs checksums.txt with the private key, producing checksums.txt.sig
4. Add the public key hex to LDFLAGS: -X github.com/OpenScribbler/syllago/cli/internal/updater.SigningPublicKey=<hex>
5. Upload checksums.txt.sig as a release artifact alongside checksums.txt

Note: This is separate from the existing cosign signing (which signs checksums.txt.bundle for external verification). The Ed25519 signature is for the binary's built-in self-update verification.

## Success Criteria
- Release binaries have the signing key embedded
- Self-update downloads and verifies checksums.txt.sig
- Tampered checksums are rejected
- Dev builds still work (gracefully skip verification)

## Blocked By
Repo must be public for the release workflow to run against public GitHub Releases

title: Add release environment with manual approval gate
description:
## Why
The release workflow (release.yml) has no environment protection rules. It triggers on any v* tag push and builds+signs+publishes binaries with contents:write and id-token:write permissions. No manual approval step exists between tag push and public release.

## What
Create a "release" environment in GitHub with required manual approval, and reference it in release.yml.

## How
- Settings > Environments > New environment > "release"
- Add required reviewers (holdenhewett)
- Optionally: restrict to main branch only
- In release.yml, add: environment: release (under the job definition)

## Success Criteria
- Tag push triggers the release workflow but pauses for manual approval
- Approved releases proceed to build, sign, and publish
- Rejected releases do not publish anything

## Blocked By
Repo must be public for environment protection rules (free tier)

title: Create CODEOWNERS file for security-sensitive paths
description:
## Why
No CODEOWNERS file exists. Without it, require_code_owner_reviews has nothing to enforce. Security-sensitive paths (workflows, installer, sandbox, signing) should require explicit review from maintainers.

## What
Create .github/CODEOWNERS with ownership rules for critical paths.

## How
Create .github/CODEOWNERS with at minimum:
- /.github/workflows/ @holdenhewett
- /cli/internal/installer/ @holdenhewett
- /cli/internal/sandbox/ @holdenhewett
- /cli/internal/signing/ @holdenhewett
- /cli/internal/updater/ @holdenhewett
- /cli/internal/registry/ @holdenhewett
- /SECURITY.md @holdenhewett
Then enable require_code_owner_reviews in branch protection.

## Success Criteria
- PRs touching security-sensitive paths require review from @holdenhewett
- CODEOWNERS file is checked into the repo

## Blocked By
Repo must be public — CODEOWNERS enforcement requires branch protection features

title: Enable dismiss stale PR reviews on main
description:
## Why
dismiss_stale_reviews is false. A reviewer can approve a PR, then the author can push entirely new code and merge without re-approval. Combined with require_last_push_approval being false, the author can self-approve and merge.

## What
Enable dismiss_stale_reviews and require_last_push_approval on the main branch protection rule.

## How
- Settings > Branches > main > Require approvals > Enable "Dismiss stale pull request approvals when new commits are pushed"
- Enable "Require approval from someone other than the last pusher"

## Success Criteria
- Stale approvals are dismissed when new commits are pushed
- Last pusher cannot be the approving reviewer

## Blocked By
Repo must be public for full effect with external contributors

title: M7: .syllago/ state files not gitignored
description:
## Why
.syllago/audit.jsonl, .syllago/installed.json, and .syllago/snapshots/ contain machine-local state (audit events with timestamps, absolute paths, install history) but aren't gitignored. If committed, they expose development activity patterns and filesystem structure. The syllago repo itself currently has these files showing up in git status.

## What
Add .syllago/ state files to .gitignore and clean up any already-tracked instances.

## How (general)
- Add to .gitignore: .syllago/audit.jsonl, .syllago/installed.json, .syllago/snapshots/
- Consider adding .syllago/ entirely (config.json is also environment-specific)
- If .syllago.yaml needs to remain tracked (it's content metadata), be specific about which files to ignore
- Clean up already-tracked files: git rm --cached for any tracked state files
- Also add cli/.syllago/audit.jsonl pattern for test artifacts

## Success Criteria
- git status shows no .syllago/audit.jsonl or .syllago/installed.json as modified/untracked
- .syllago.yaml files (content metadata) remain trackable
- Test artifacts in cli/.syllago/ are also ignored

## Testing
- After changes: git status confirms state files are ignored
- New clone: verify .syllago/ state files are not present
- syllago init: verify it creates state files that are git-ignored

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M7
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: M5: JSONC comments destroyed during merge
description:
## Why
For OpenCode, JSONC comments are stripped before merge and the clean JSON is written back — comments are permanently lost. For Zed (which uses JSONC natively in settings.json), no stripping is applied at all, so sjson may produce corrupt output. Either syllago corrupts the file or destroys user comments.

## What
Handle JSONC-format settings files correctly for providers that use them (Zed, OpenCode).

## How (general)
- Option A (preferred): Apply StripJSONCComments universally in readJSONFile and document that comments will be removed. This is honest and predictable.
- Option B: Use a JSONC-aware JSON manipulation library that preserves comments during merge.
- Option C: For providers with JSONC settings (Zed), refuse to merge and ask the user to configure a dedicated MCP file. Document the limitation.
- At minimum: fix the Zed case where no stripping happens and sjson gets JSONC input (likely produces corrupt output)

## Success Criteria
- Zed settings.json with comments does not get corrupted by MCP merge
- Users are either warned about comment loss or comments are preserved
- All provider merge paths handle JSONC consistently

## Testing
- Unit test: merge into settings file with // comments — verify no corruption
- Unit test: merge into settings file with /* */ block comments — verify no corruption
- Unit test: merge into settings file with trailing commas — verify handled
- Test with actual Zed settings.json format

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M5
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: M6: Absolute paths leak through promote pipeline
description:
## Why
BundledScriptMeta.OriginalPath (in metadata.go:33) stores the absolute filesystem path at add time (e.g., /home/username/.claude/hooks/lint.sh). This field survives copyForPromote and ends up in .syllago.yaml files in public registries, revealing the original user's home directory path, username, and filesystem layout.

## What
Strip or relativize OriginalPath when promoting content to registries.

## How (general)
- In the promote pipeline's copyForPromote function, sanitize .syllago.yaml before committing to the registry branch
- Replace absolute OriginalPath with just the filename (filepath.Base) or a relative path
- Consider whether OriginalPath is even needed in the registry copy — if it's only used for local tracking, it can be removed entirely during promote
- Also audit other metadata fields for absolute path leakage

## Success Criteria
- Promoted content in registries contains no absolute filesystem paths
- No username or home directory information leaks through .syllago.yaml
- Local library copies retain OriginalPath for local use (no behavior change locally)

## Testing
- Unit test: promote content with BundledScriptMeta, verify OriginalPath is sanitized in registry copy
- Unit test: verify local library copy still has full OriginalPath
- Integration test: promote → clone registry → inspect .syllago.yaml for path leakage
- Grep test: no absolute paths matching /home/ or /Users/ in promoted output

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M6
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: M2: Hook security scanner not called during install
description:
## Why
hook_security.go has a regex-based scanner for dangerous commands (curl, wget, nc, rm -rf) but installHook() never calls it. The scanner only runs during import/convert. Registry content can change between import and install (registry updates). The last chance to warn the user is at install time, and it's skipped.

## What
Wire ScanHookSecurity into the install path so users get warnings before hooks are written to provider settings.

## How (general)
- In installer/hooks.go installHook(), call converter.ScanHookSecurity on the hook command before writing to settings
- Display warnings to the user with finding details
- Consider requiring --allow-unsafe flag for high-severity findings
- Don't block on info-level findings, only warn

## Success Criteria
- Installing a hook with dangerous commands (curl, wget, etc.) shows security warnings
- Warnings include the specific dangerous command found and its severity
- Installation proceeds with user acknowledgment or --allow-unsafe flag
- Normal hooks install without interruption

## Testing
- Unit test: install hook with curl command, verify warning is emitted
- Unit test: install hook with safe command, verify no warning
- Unit test: --allow-unsafe flag suppresses blocking on warnings
- Table-driven tests for each pattern in the scanner

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M2
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: M4: Hook matcher groups not whitelist-filtered before merge
description:
## Why
The hook matcher group (everything except the "event" field) is inserted as raw JSON into settings.json. Unlike MCP install (which roundtrips through a typed struct to strip unknown fields), hooks preserve ALL JSON fields including unknown ones. A crafted hook.json could inject arbitrary keys into the provider's settings.json.

## What
Parse hook matcher groups through a typed struct before merging, matching the MCP whitelist approach.

## How (general)
- Define a typed struct for hook matcher groups with known fields (matcher, command, timeout, etc.)
- In installHook(), unmarshal the raw matcher group JSON into the typed struct
- Re-marshal the struct (which strips unknown fields) before passing to sjson.SetRawBytes
- This matches the pattern already used in MCP's ExtractServerEntries

## Success Criteria
- Known hook fields (matcher, command, timeout) are preserved during install
- Unknown/unexpected JSON fields are stripped before writing to settings.json
- Installed hooks function correctly with providers

## Testing
- Unit test: hook with only known fields — all preserved
- Unit test: hook with extra unknown fields — unknown fields stripped
- Unit test: hook with all supported matcher fields — all preserved
- Golden test: compare installed hook JSON before/after whitelist filtering

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M4
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: M3: Hook event name used as sjson key without validation
description:
## Why
The event name from hook JSON is used directly in "hooks." + event + ".-1" as an sjson key path. Unlike MCP server names (validated by IsValidItemName), event names aren't validated. An event name containing dots (e.g., "PreToolUse.evil.path") causes sjson to create nested JSON structures instead of flat keys, corrupting settings.json.

## What
Validate hook event names before using them as sjson key paths.

## How (general)
- Add event name validation in installHook() before constructing the sjson key path
- Option A: Validate against a whitelist of known provider event names (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, etc.)
- Option B: Ensure the event string contains no dots or special characters that sjson interprets
- Option A is preferred — it also catches typos and invalid event names early

## Success Criteria
- Hook files with invalid event names are rejected with a clear error
- Hook files with dots in event names are rejected (sjson key injection)
- All known provider event names pass validation
- Error message tells the user what valid event names look like

## Testing
- Unit test: install hook with valid event name (PreToolUse) — succeeds
- Unit test: install hook with dotted event name — rejected
- Unit test: install hook with empty event name — rejected
- Unit test: install hook with each known provider event name — all succeed

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — Section M3
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

**Effort:** S (< 30 min)

Replace direct os.Exit calls with osExit(). In tests, override:
  orig := osExit
  osExit = func(code int) { /* capture code */ }

Once the seam is in place, runDoctor becomes a standard Cobra RunE that calls individual check* functions (most already tested). Test the orchestration: all-pass, some-warn, some-fail scenarios.
