title: Hook security warnings during import
description:
WHY: The hooks deep-dive (docs/providers/audit/hooks.md lines 126-131) highlights that project-level hooks are an attack surface. A malicious .claude/settings.json in a cloned repo could execute arbitrary commands. syllago should warn when importing hooks that contain potentially dangerous commands.

RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (Security section, lines 126-131)
RESEARCH REF: Community projects: lasso-security/claude-hooks (prompt injection defense), affaan-m/everything-claude-code (config protection hooks)

WHAT TO BUILD:
- During syllago add of hooks content, scan hook commands for:
  - Shell commands that access network (curl, wget, nc)
  - Commands that modify system state (rm, chmod, chown)
  - Commands that read secrets (cat ~/.ssh/*, env | grep)
  - Overly broad matchers (match everything)
- Produce warnings (not blocks) with explanations
- Optional --security-scan flag for deeper analysis

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Importing hooks with 'curl' commands produces a security warning
- Importing hooks with narrow matchers and safe commands produces no warning
- Warnings are informational, not blocking

TESTING: Unit tests with various hook commands
close_reason: ScanHookSecurity scanner: HIGH (network/destructive), MEDIUM (broad matchers/permissions), LOW (system paths). 20 test cases. All tests pass.

title: syllago compat --hooks: show provider hook capability matrix
description:
WHY: Users need to quickly see which hook features are supported where before writing cross-provider hooks. The hooks deep-dive (docs/providers/audit/hooks.md) contains a detailed capability comparison that should be surfaced in the CLI.

RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (capability table, lines 46-59)

WHAT TO BUILD:
- New --hooks flag on syllago compat (or syllago info --hooks)
- Renders a table showing: events supported, can block, can modify args, handler types, maturity level
- Per-provider breakdown
- Could also show event name mappings (PreToolUse → BeforeTool → pre_run_command etc.)

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Command produces readable capability matrix
- Data matches current provider research
- Updates when new providers/events are added to toolmap

TESTING: Unit test for output format, integration test for data accuracy

title: Preserve updatedInput and structured output fields in hook conversion
description:
WHY: Claude Code hooks can return structured JSON output including updatedInput (transparent arg rewriting), suppressOutput, systemMessage, additionalContext, continue, and permissionDecision. These fields are critical for security hooks. Our converter preserves the hook definition but doesn't model or warn about these runtime capabilities.

RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (lines 30-31 — structured output fields)
RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/claude-code/hooks.md (exit codes and structured output)
RELATED: syllago-qumq (hook matcher regex handling) — could be combined

WHAT TO DO:
- Document which structured output fields each provider supports
- During hook conversion, if a hook's purpose implies arg rewriting (e.g., command rewrites tool input), warn when exporting to providers that don't support updatedInput
- Consider adding a 'capabilities' annotation to canonical hook entries

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Converter warns about capability mismatches for structured output
- Documentation of per-provider structured output support

TESTING: Unit tests for warning conditions
close_reason: HookOutputCapabilities map for 6 providers. SourceProvider propagation in canonical. OutputFieldsLostWarnings generates mismatch warnings. 8 new tests. All tests pass.

title: Hook portability report: warn about capability mismatches during install/export
description:
WHY: Different providers have vastly different hook capabilities. A hook using updatedInput (arg rewriting) only works on Claude Code and Gemini CLI — Windsurf/Cursor silently ignore the rewrite. A hook using 'prompt' handler type only works on Claude Code. Users exporting hooks cross-provider get no warning about these capability losses.

RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (capability matrix on lines 46-59)
RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/claude-code/hooks.md (4 handler types)
RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/windsurf/hooks.md (command-only handlers)

WHAT TO BUILD:
- During syllago install/export of hooks, analyze hook entries for provider-specific features
- Warn about: handler types not supported by target (http/prompt/agent → command-only providers), updatedInput not supported (only CC/Gemini), async execution not supported, matcher syntax differences
- Could be part of the broader syllago compat command (syllago-u6b6) or standalone

CAPABILITY MATRIX TO ENCODE:
| Capability | CC | Gemini | Windsurf | Cursor | Cline | Copilot | Codex | Kiro |
| Block tool calls | Y | Y | Y | Y (3/6) | Y (PreToolUse) | Y (preToolUse) | N | Y (CLI) |
| Modify args | Y (updatedInput) | Y (rewrite) | N | N | Y (context inject) | Y | N | N |
| Handler types | cmd,http,prompt,agent | cmd | cmd | cmd | executable | cmd | cmd | askAgent+cmd |
| Async execution | Y | N | N | N | N | N | N | N |

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Installing a hook with 'type: http' to Windsurf produces a warning
- Installing a hook with updatedInput to Cursor produces a warning
- No false warnings for compatible features

TESTING: Unit tests for each warning condition

title: Fix hook matcher regex handling in translation
description:
WHY: Matchers like 'Edit|Write' or 'mcp__github__.*' are passed to TranslateTool as single strings, breaking regex patterns. The matcher should have its tool name components translated individually while preserving the regex syntax.

FINDINGS:

SUCCESS CRITERIA:

- Wildcard matchers (.*) preserved
- MCP prefix patterns handled correctly
- Simple string matchers still work

Makes the provider research process repeatable without re-explaining. Depends on completing the initial provider research (44-agent audit).

title: Cross-provider skill hook conversion
description:
When converting Claude Code skills with hooks frontmatter to other providers, map skill-scoped hooks to the target provider's hook system where possible.

Current state: hooks and effort fields are preserved in SkillMeta (canonical format) and survive Claude-to-Claude roundtrips. For non-Claude providers, hooks are embedded as prose notes explaining they require skill-scoped hook support.

Future work:
- Map Claude skill hooks to Gemini CLI global hooks in settings.json (lose skill scoping but hooks actually execute)
- Map hook event types between providers (e.g., pre_tool_use → BeforeTool)
- Handle scoping semantics (Claude: per-skill, Gemini: global, Copilot: none)
- Decide on warning/confirmation UX when hook scoping is lost

Research notes:
- Gemini CLI has 10 hook event types, configured in settings.json, all global scope
- Copilot CLI has no hook system
- Kiro has hooks but different event model
- The core challenge is that hooks are executable behavior, not instructional text — prose embedding preserves intent but hooks won't fire
close_reason: Skill hooks surfaced as actionable warnings for providers with hook support. Providers without hooks keep prose embedding. Tests pass.

title: Warn on empty registry repos
description:
## Phase 1e: Warn on empty registry repos

### Problem
app.go:247-249 has a comment "No content at all — warn but allow" but never actually warns. Registry gets added with 0 items and no feedback about being empty.

### Implementation
Change the success message at that code path to indicate emptiness:
```go
"Added registry: %s (empty — no content found)"
```
instead of the normal "Added registry: %s" message.

### Files
- `cli/internal/tui/app.go` around line 247-249

### Success Criteria
- [ ] When a registry is added that has no content, the success message includes "(empty — no content found)"
- [ ] Normal (non-empty) registry adds still show the standard message without the empty warning
- [ ] `make build && make test` passes

### DO NOT
- Do not mark this bead as complete — a verification agent will do that
- Do not change the behavior (still allow adding empty registries) — just change the message
close_reason: Closed

title: Full TUI codebase review: efficiency, accessibility, best practices
description:
## Goal
Comprehensive review of the full TUI codebase (cli/internal/tui/) by Go CLI expert agents, evaluating against current best practices for:
- Efficiency and code organization (state management, rendering, TEA architecture)
- CLI best practices (error messages, user feedback, progressive disclosure)
- CLI accessibility (NO_COLOR, screen readers, keyboard nav, color-independent meaning)
- TUI accessibility (focus management, modal patterns, status regions, form inputs)
- TUI patterns and best practices (modals, async UX, navigation, card layouts)

## Requirements
- Spin up research agents for each topic area to gather current (2024-2026) authoritative sources
- Feed research into Go expert agents that review the actual codebase files
- Produce both high-level pattern findings AND specific file:line recommendations
- Review scope: all files in cli/internal/tui/ plus supporting packages they depend on

## Success Criteria
- Each topic area has a structured findings document with citations
- Actionable recommendations are tied to specific code locations
- Findings are saved to docs/ for reference during implementation
- Critical issues (accessibility failures, UX anti-patterns) are separated from nice-to-haves
close_reason: Review complete: all research and findings docs exist in docs/reviews/ and docs/research-*. Implementation plan with 68 items across 6 phases documented. Many items already implemented in prior sessions.

title: Formal interchange specs for content types (if community demand emerges)
description:
## Why
The hooks spec (docs/spec/hooks/hooks.md) is full standards-track fidelity: capability matrices, conformance levels, test vectors, security considerations, schema. That level of effort is justified ONLY if other tools want to be syllago-compatible — i.e., if external community demand for formal interchange specs materializes.

For internal/external user needs (understanding what syllago does, replicating its work without reading Go code), the cheat-sheet reference docs (syllago-shhco) are sufficient.

## What
Promote the canonical format reference docs into hooks.md-style formal specs for any content type where external demand exists. Six potential candidates:
1. skills
2. MCP
3. agents
4. rules
5. commands
6. loadouts

## When to act on this
Don't proactively work on this. Trigger conditions:
- External tool author asks for a formal spec to implement against
- Agent Ecosystem org or similar community body wants syllago canonical formats as standards-track
- Multiple syllago users hit ambiguity in the cheat-sheet reference docs

## Scope decisions deferred until then
- Whether specs live in syllago repo or move to a community org (per project_agent_ecosystem_spec memory)
- Whether to match hooks.md fidelity exactly or pick a lighter middle ground
- Sequencing (probably skills last — its canonical format is in flux)

## Replaces
Post-release half of split syllago-quf0.

title: Org-config staleness detection in CI
description: When org-config deny-scan-as entries are updated by IT but CI runner images haven't been rebuilt, pipelines run stale security boundaries. Could surface a warning if org-config mtime predates a configurable threshold. Panel review: Marcus's runner-image-lag concern.

title: JSON output for 500-file cap warning
description: When the 500-file candidate cap is hit during content-signal detection, the warning should be available in machine-readable JSON format for pipeline parsing. Currently a soft warning, easily elevated. Panel review N5.
close_reason: Promoted from N5 to I8 in design doc — now part of implementation plan scope, not a separate roadmap item

title: User identity in confirm audit log entries
description: Audit log entries for confirmed items should include the authenticated user identity, not just a timestamp. Required for compliance in regulated environments. Panel review N4.

title: Converter field manifest for admin review
description: Converters should declare their contributed classification fields in a machine-readable manifest so administrators can review what field vocabularies are registered without reading source code. Lower priority now that dynamic signals don't affect scoring, but valuable for long-term governance transparency. Panel review N2.

title: Cap dynamic signal contribution in audit display
description: Even with dynamic signals excluded from threshold scoring (B3), the audit log display should cap total dynamic field contribution per file (e.g., max +0.20 from field matches) to prevent bloated converter registrations from producing misleading displayed scores. Panel review N1.

title: Generated file PR noise reduction (.gitattributes linguist-generated)
description:
When syllago generates/installs files that get committed to repos, they create PR noise. Add .gitattributes entries marking generated files as linguist-generated so GitHub collapses them in PR diffs.

RULESYNC VALIDATION: Issue #1224 — users requested this exact feature. Generated files can't be gitignored (tools need them to exist) but they flood PRs with noise.

SYLLAGO APPROACH: When syllago installs content, optionally update .gitattributes with:
  .claude/rules/* linguist-generated
  CLAUDE.md linguist-generated
  (etc. per provider)

This is a small DX win that shows polish. Low priority but easy to implement.

title: Permissions as a content type
Simulated features for non-native providers
description:
rulesync generates 'simulated' skills/commands/subagents for providers that don't natively support them, embedding prompt-engineering text in the root rule file.

RULESYNC VALIDATION: Issue #902 requests ephemeral generation (rulesync run without persisting files) — shows users want even MORE flexibility in how simulated content is delivered. But no issues praising or complaining about simulation quality, which suggests it either works quietly or isn't widely used.

Issue #900 reveals a deeper problem: path references in rules aren't transformed during generation. If a rule says 'see @.rulesync/rules/example.md', it stays as-is in the generated output — broken link. This affects simulated features too since they reference skill files by path.

SYLLAGO APPROACH: Don't simulate by default. Instead:
1. When converting content to a provider that lacks native support, emit a clear WARNING telling the user what was dropped and why
2. Offer an opt-in --simulate flag that generates prompt-engineered fallbacks
3. If we do simulate, solve the path reference problem rulesync hasn't — rewrite paths to point to the correct provider-relative locations

This keeps the default behavior honest (you know what your provider actually supports) while offering simulation as an explicit choice. Rulesync's silent simulation means users might not realize their "skills" on Copilot are just prompt suggestions, not native features.

PRIORITY: Post-release. Focus on native support coverage first.

title: Research: import heuristics as fallback for untyped content
L7: installed.json/settings.json crash window between writes
description:
## Why
For hooks, settings.json is written first, then installed.json. If the process crashes between these two writes, the hook is in settings.json but not tracked in installed.json — an orphan that syllago can't uninstall. Same pattern in MCP install.

## What
Document the recovery procedure. A full fix (write-ahead log) is over-engineering for this narrow crash window.

## How (general)
- Add a recovery note to syllago doctor output: detect hooks/MCP entries in settings.json not tracked by installed.json
- doctor command already exists — add an orphan detection check
- Optionally: offer to clean up orphaned entries

## Success Criteria
- syllago doctor detects orphaned hooks/MCP entries
- User guidance on how to resolve orphans (manual removal or doctor --fix)

## Testing
- Unit test: create orphaned hook entry in settings.json (not in installed.json), run doctor — detected
- Unit test: all entries tracked — doctor reports clean

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — L7
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: L5: CleanStale removes dirs by name prefix without uid check
description:
## Why
CleanStale iterates /tmp for directories named syllago-sandbox-* and removes them with os.RemoveAll. On shared systems, another user's legitimately-named directory could be deleted. Inner symlinks within a real directory would be followed by RemoveAll.

## What
Verify directory ownership before removal in CleanStale.

## How (general)
- After Lstat, check that the directory is owned by the current uid (os.Getuid())
- Use stat.Sys().(*syscall.Stat_t).Uid on Linux to get owner
- Skip directories not owned by current user

## Success Criteria
- Only directories owned by the current user are cleaned
- Other users' syllago-sandbox-* directories are left alone

## Testing
- Unit test: directory owned by current user — removed
- Unit test: directory owned by different user — skipped (may need root to test, or mock)

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — L5
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: L6: Hook script symlink not resolved before containment check
description:
## Why
resolveHookScripts() uses filepath.Join + filepath.Rel to check that script paths stay within the item directory, but doesn't resolve symlinks first. A symlink in the item directory pointing outside could pass the string-based containment check but read from an arbitrary location.

## What
Add filepath.EvalSymlinks before the containment check in resolveHookScripts.

## How (general)
- After filepath.Clean(filepath.Join(itemDir, scriptPath)), call filepath.EvalSymlinks
- Then run the filepath.Rel containment check against the resolved path
- This matches the pattern used in dirsafety.go:49

## Success Criteria
- Symlinks pointing outside the item directory are rejected
- Normal relative script paths still resolve correctly

## Testing
- Unit test: script path is a regular file within item dir — succeeds
- Unit test: script path is a symlink to file outside item dir — rejected
- Unit test: script path with .. that resolves within item dir — succeeds

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — L6
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: L3: User-configured provider paths not bounds-checked
description:
## Why
PathResolver.InstallDir() can return a per-type path from user config (providerPaths in .syllago.yaml). The path is cleaned with filepath.Clean but never validated to be within a reasonable scope. If the config is shared (team repo), a malicious config could direct writes to sensitive locations.

## What
Validate resolved provider paths against a safe directory set.

## How (general)
- In resolver.go InstallDir(), validate that returned paths are within the user's home directory
- Block paths to /, /etc, /tmp, /var, etc. (similar to sensitiveBlocklist in dirsafety.go)
- Log a warning when provider paths are overridden from config

## Success Criteria
- Provider paths pointing outside home directory are rejected
- Normal home-directory provider paths work as before
- Clear error message when path validation fails

## Testing
- Unit test: provider path within home dir — allowed
- Unit test: provider path to /etc — rejected
- Unit test: provider path to / — rejected

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — L3
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: L1: Symlink creation TOCTOU race in CreateSymlink
description:
## Why
CreateSymlink checks if the target exists with Lstat, removes it, then creates a new symlink. Between Remove and Symlink, another process could place a symlink at the target path. Requires attacker write access to provider config dir (low practical risk).

## What
Use atomic symlink replacement pattern (create temp symlink, then rename).

## How (general)
- Create a temporary symlink with a random name in the same directory
- Use os.Rename to atomically replace the target with the temp symlink
- os.Rename atomically replaces the target, closing the TOCTOU window

## Success Criteria
- Symlink creation is atomic (no window between remove and create)
- Existing symlinks are replaced without race condition

## Testing
- Unit test: concurrent symlink creation doesn't produce errors
- Existing symlink tests continue to pass

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — L1
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: L2: Uninstall RemoveAll without scope validation
description:
## Why
Uninstall() calls os.RemoveAll(targetPath) where targetPath comes from provider.InstallDir + filepath.Base(item.Path). No validation that targetPath is within the expected install directory tree. If a PathResolver is misconfigured, RemoveAll could delete outside scope.

## What
Add containment check before RemoveAll in uninstall path.

## How (general)
- Before RemoveAll, verify targetPath starts with the expected installDir prefix using filepath.Rel
- Reject paths that escape the install directory
- Similar pattern to the containment check in hooks.go:321-326

## Success Criteria
- Uninstall refuses to remove paths outside the expected install directory
- Normal uninstall operations are unaffected

## Testing
- Unit test: uninstall with path inside install dir — succeeds
- Unit test: uninstall with path outside install dir — rejected with error

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — L2
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: L4: MCP backup is non-atomic and single-depth
description:
## Why
backupFile uses os.WriteFile directly (not atomic temp-then-rename). Only one .bak is kept — overwritten on each operation. If crash during backup write, the .bak is corrupted. Hooks already use a proper snapshot system.

## What
Extend snapshot-based rollback to MCP operations (matching hooks).

## How (general)
- Option A: Use the same snapshot system hooks use (snapshot.CreateForHook equivalent for MCP)
- Option B: At minimum, make backupFile use the atomic write pattern (writeJSONFileWithPerm)
- Option C: Keep .bak but make it atomic and add rotation (keep last N backups)

## Success Criteria
- MCP backup files survive power loss / crash during write
- Multiple sequential operations don't destroy earlier recovery points

## Testing
- Unit test: verify backup file is written atomically
- Unit test: verify snapshot exists after MCP install

## Threat Report
docs/security/threat-inventory-2026-03-26.md — L4
close_reason: Implemented in security hardening session 2026-03-26

title: Cache sidebar counts to avoid 7x catalog iteration
description:
refreshSidebarCounts() iterates all 7 content types, calling a.catalog.ByType(ct) for each (which loops the full catalog), then a.visibleItems() for each result (another loop). Total: 14+ passes over the catalog on every sidebar refresh.

Fix: Compute all counts in a single pass over a.catalog.Items, accumulating into a map[ContentType]int. Only invalidate when catalog data changes (install/remove/toggle-hidden), not on every UI refresh.

Files: cli/internal/tui/app.go (refreshSidebarCounts)

title: Research assistantkit/hooks Go package as prior art
description:
WHY: The hooks deep-dive (docs/providers/audit/hooks.md line 107) mentions assistantkit/hooks, a Go package published March 2026 that provides canonical event types and adapter interfaces for converting between Claude Code, Cursor, and Windsurf hook formats. This is doing exactly what syllago's hook converter does. We should study it for: alignment opportunities, patterns we can adopt, potential contribution or integration.

RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (line 107-108)

WHAT TO DO:
- Find and review the assistantkit/hooks repo
- Compare their canonical event types against our HookEvents map
- Compare their adapter interfaces against our Canonicalize/Render pattern
- Document findings: what they do better, what we do better, alignment opportunities
- Decision: adopt patterns, contribute back, or stay independent

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Written comparison doc in docs/providers/
- Clear recommendation on whether to adopt, align, or ignore

TESTING: N/A — research task

title: Hook structural translation: Windsurf per-tool events ↔ event+matcher
description:
WHY: Windsurf uses per-tool-category events (pre_read_code, pre_write_code, pre_run_command) while Claude Code/Gemini use generic event+matcher (PreToolUse + matcher: 'Bash'). Currently we route Windsurf through the standard converter with a TODO. Full structural translation would enable true cross-provider hook portability.

RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (Windsurf section, lines 71)
RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/windsurf/hooks.md (12 hook events)
AUDIT REF: docs/providers/audit/windsurf.md (WS-7: structural mismatch)

WHAT TO BUILD:
- Canonicalize: Windsurf pre_read_code → PreToolUse + matcher: 'Read'
- Canonicalize: Windsurf pre_write_code → PreToolUse + matcher: 'Write|Edit'
- Canonicalize: Windsurf pre_run_command → PreToolUse + matcher: 'Bash'
- Render: PreToolUse + matcher: 'Bash' → Windsurf pre_run_command
- Handle unmappable events (post_cascade_response_with_transcript is Windsurf-only)

ALSO: Cline file-based executables ↔ JSON config translation

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- Round-trip: Windsurf hooks → canonical → Claude Code → canonical → Windsurf preserves semantics
- Warnings for unmappable events

TESTING: Round-trip tests for each event type

title: Cross-platform hook template library in registries
description:
WHY: The hooks ecosystem is fragmented (docs/providers/audit/hooks.md conclusion). Community hook collections cluster around individual tools (karanb192/claude-code-hooks, github/awesome-copilot, etc.). syllago registries could host cross-platform hook templates that work across providers with automatic format conversion.

RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (community collections, lines 111-112)
RESEARCH REF: docs/providers/audit/hooks.md (no cross-platform standard, lines 103-108)

WHAT TO BUILD:
- Hook templates in syllago canonical format in registries
- syllago install of hook templates auto-converts to target provider format
- Common templates: security enforcement (block dangerous commands), auto-format, auto-test, git workflow, notification
- Each template includes provider-aware fallbacks for missing capabilities

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
- At least 5 hook templates installable to any hooks-capable provider
- Templates degrade gracefully on providers with fewer capabilities

TESTING: Integration tests installing templates to multiple providers

title: Explore override flag for private→public content sharing
Roadmap: Copilot CLI smoke tests
description:
# Copilot CLI Smoke Tests (Roadmap)

## Why
GitHub Copilot CLI's programmatic mode needs research before we can write smoke tests. Deferred from the initial E2E test suite.

## To Research
- What programmatic/non-interactive flags does Copilot CLI support?
- How does Copilot CLI surface loaded rules/instructions?
- What authentication mechanism works for CI runners?
- Is there a config introspection command (like claude mcp list)?

## When Ready
Follow the same pattern as Claude Code and Gemini CLI smoke tests.
